questions.question: What is the primary advantage of using a singly linked list over an array?
questions.explanation: Singly linked lists allow for dynamic size, making them more flexible than arrays.

questions.question: Which of the following operations can be performed on a singly linked list in constant time?
questions.explanation: Deleting at the end of a linked list can be done in constant time because it only involves updating the previous node's pointer.

questions.question: What is the name of the operation that traverses the linked list from the beginning to the end?
questions.explanation: Traversing the linked list from the head to the tail is the most common type of traversal.

questions.question: Why is self-referential structure used in linked lists?
options: To allow nodes to point to other nodes
questions.explanation: Self-referential structures enable nodes to point to other nodes, allowing for more complex operations and data structures.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'next' pointer in a linked list?
options: To keep track of the node's value
To point to the next node in the list
To keep track of the node's position
questions.explanation: The 'next' pointer points to the next node in the list, enabling traversal and insertion/deletion operations.
